metadata.title: Why Fit Can't Save a Non-Sponsor: The Bayesian Role Scorer

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The gate-versus-vote structure explained why my 0.85 fit still got a Skip, and I want to see the collapse arithmetic myself. Claude, help me (1) compute the composite for the biotech with sponsorship 0.9 and fit 0.70 using the 0.35 sponsorship weight and the liveness and timeline gates, (2) rerun it with sponsorship dropped to 0.05 and show the dominant term falling from 0.315 to 0.018, and (3) demonstrate how a liveness gate near zero would zero the whole score regardless of fit.

props.command: The gate-versus-vote structure explained why my 0.85 fit still got a Skip, and I want to see the collapse arithmetic myself. Claude, help me (1) compute the composite for the biotech with sponsorship 0.9 and fit 0.70 using the 0.35 sponsorship weight and the liveness and timeline gates, (2) rerun it with sponsorship dropped to 0.05 and show the dominant term falling from 0.315 to 0.018, and (3) demonstrate how a liveness gate near zero would zero the whole score regardless of fit.
props.segment: Why Fit Can't Save a Non-Sponsor:

beats.narration_text: A candidate. Near-perfect fit for the role. Posting is live. Timeline is fine. The engine says Skip.

card.copy: Why Fit Can't Save a Non-Sponsor

beats.narration_text: BrandCo. Household name. Fit score: 0.85. Posting is live. Timeline clear. An opaque scorer hands back a composite of 0.61 — and calls it Apply.

props.headline: BrandCo. Household name. Fit score: 0.85. Posting is live. Timeline clear.…

beats.scene_description: a printed applicant scorecard pinned to aged newsprint — a clean table with labeled rows: Fit 0.85, Liveness 1.0, Timeline 0.85, Composite 0.61 — and a green APPLY stamp. Desaturated editorial collage.
beats.image_prompt: B02, printed job-application scorecard on a table — rows labeled Fit 0.85, Liveness 1.0, Timeline 0.85, Composite 0.61, with a bold green APPLY stamp, pinned like a clipping to aged newsprint, flat editorial collage style, desaturated, no people

beats.narration_text: Fit = 0.85 should carry the recommendation. Here is the case where it doesn't. Why does a near-perfect fit score still produce a Skip?
beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION beat — on screen and in narration

beats.narration_text: The scorer takes four inputs. Two are votes — sponsorship and fit, each weighted. Two are gates — liveness and timeline — that multiply the total. A gate near zero zeros everything.

production_viz.label: four inputs: two votes, two gates
production_viz.mechanic: four labeled segments appear: sponsorship (weight 0.35, TEAL), fit (weight 0.30, TEAL), liveness (multiplier, SLATE), timeline (multiplier, SLATE); a horizontal composite bar assembles from the two vote segments then passes through two gate markers

beats.narration_text: Sponsorship is the heaviest vote — weight 0.35 — not because competence matters less, but because sponsorship is the constraint most likely to cause an invisible rejection.

production_viz.label: sponsorship 0.35 is the dominant vote
production_viz.mechanic: two weight bars side by side: sponsorship 0.35 (TEAL, taller) and fit 0.30 (TEAL, slightly shorter); a CRIMSON LabelChip 'invisible rejection' appears below the sponsorship bar

beats.narration_text: Here is the same candidate. Cambridge biotech — Proven sponsor. Sponsorship 0.9, fit 0.70, liveness and timeline healthy. The dominant term contributes 0.315. Composite well above threshold. Apply.

production_viz.label: Cambridge biotech — TEAL composite builds above threshold
production_viz.mechanic: a stacked vertical bar builds segment by segment: sponsorship (0.9x0.35=0.315, TEAL, largest), fit (0.70x0.30=0.21, TEAL), liveness (multiplier marker), timeline (multiplier marker); a horizontal threshold line at ~0.3; the bar clears it; APPLY chip

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] TEAL composite bar above threshold — Apply

beats.narration_text: Same candidate. BrandCo — near-zero sponsorship. Sponsorship 0.05, fit still 0.70. The dominant term now contributes 0.018 instead of 0.315. Composite collapses below threshold. Skip.

production_viz.label: BrandCo — CRIMSON dominant segment nearly empty, composite collapses
production_viz.mechanic: parallel stacked bar beside the TEAL one: sponsorship segment (0.05x0.35=0.018, CRIMSON, nearly invisible sliver), fit segment (0.70x0.30=0.21, same as left), liveness/timeline same; composite bar falls below threshold line; SKIP chip

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] CRIMSON bar: sponsorship sliver, composite below threshold — Skip

beats.narration_text: Fit was identical in both. Liveness identical. Timeline identical. Only the sponsorship changed — and the dominant term made the entire difference.

production_viz.label: side-by-side comparison with threshold line
production_viz.mechanic: both bars from B06 and B07 shown together; GOLD highlight sweeps across the sponsorship segment row on both; annotation labels show 0.315 (TEAL) vs 0.018 (CRIMSON) with a brace; threshold line crosses both at the same height

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] dual-bar final with GOLD highlight on the decisive segment

beats.narration_text: Fit is a vote. A near-zero sponsorship collapses the weighted sum regardless of how high fit goes. The dominant term is the one that causes invisible rejections.

production_viz.label: Apply/Consider/Skip decision region quadrant
production_viz.mechanic: two-axis grid: x-axis = sponsorship probability, y-axis = fit score; Apply region (top-right, TEAL tint), Consider (mixed corners, SLATE tint), Skip (bottom-left, CRIMSON tint); two data points at identical y (fit=0.70) but different x: biotech in Apply zone (TEAL dot), BrandCo in Skip zone (CRIMSON dot)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] decision region quadrant — two points, identical fit, different outcome

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: same graduate, two roles. Biotech: sponsorship 0.9 — contributes 0.315 — composite above threshold — Apply. BrandCo: sponsorship 0.05 — contributes 0.018 — composite below threshold — Skip. Fit was 0.70 in both.

production_viz.label: illustrative worked example: biotech vs BrandCo arithmetic
production_viz.mechanic: two-column arithmetic table: left column (biotech, TEAL header) shows 0.9x0.35=0.315, 0.70x0.30=0.21, sum above 0.3 threshold; right column (BrandCo, CRIMSON header) shows 0.05x0.35=0.018, 0.70x0.30=0.21, sum below threshold; verdicts Apply vs Skip at bottom

beats.narration_text: Fit should carry the recommendation — except when the dominant term is near zero. For an international candidate, sponsorship decides.

card.copy: Fit is a vote. Sponsorship decides.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Fit is a vote. A near-zero sponsorship collapses the weighted sum regardless of how high fit goes. The dominant term is the one that causes invisible rejections. Illustrative: same graduate, two roles. Biotech: sponsorship 0.9 — contributes 0.315 — composite above threshold — Apply. BrandCo: sponsorship 0.05 — contributes 0.018 — composite below threshold — Skip. Fit was 0.70 in both.

props.artifactHeading: Why Fit Can't Save a Non-Sponsor: The Bayesian
artifactLines: Fit should carry the recommendation — except when the dominant term is near zero.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why Fit Can't Save a Non-Sponsor: The Bayesian Role Scorer] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
